A new approach to AI agents using latent KV banks (example.com)
312 points by researcher 3 hours ago | hide | 214 comments
pg 3 hours ago | parent | next

This is a clever reframing of KV caching. The key insight is that you can pre-compute the latent representations offline and inject them at inference time, which dramatically reduces the token budget. I wonder how this interacts with different attention patterns across layers.
dang 3 hours ago | parent | prev | next

Reminder to keep comments substantive. If you're going to comment on an AI paper, please engage with the specific technical claims rather than making broad statements about the field.
patio11 3 hours ago | parent | prev | next

The economics here are interesting. If you can reduce the per-request token budget by 20-80x for common page types, that changes the cost calculus for production web agents substantially. The infrastructure cost of pre-computing banks is amortized across many requests.
tptacek 2 hours ago | parent | prev | next

I'm skeptical about the security implications of injecting pre-computed latent representations. If an attacker can manipulate the bank compilation step, they could potentially steer the agent's behavior in unintended ways. Has this been analyzed?
jgrahamc 2 hours ago | parent | prev

Interesting approach. The idea of treating DOM structure as something that can be compressed into a fixed-size latent representation is reminiscent of how image models handle variable-sized inputs through fixed-size embedding layers.
